examples.explanation:
Starting city: 0. Permutations of [1,2]:
1) [1,2]: dist(0,1)=1 + dist(1,2)=3 + dist(2,0)=2 = 6
2) [2,1]: dist(0,2)=2 + dist(2,1)=3 + dist(1,0)=1 = 6
Minimum is 6

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n!)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of cities to visit and compute the total distance for each path, keeping track of the minimum. Use this for small n (e.g., n <= 10) due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force TSP Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of TSP using brute force permutation generation
ladders.explanation: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) asks: given n cities with distances between each pair, find the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. The brute force approach works by generating all possible permutations of city visits (excluding the starting city which is fixed), calculating the total distance for each permutation, and tracking the minimum. The intuition is that we must consider every possible ordering of cities to guarantee finding the optimal solution. Since there are (n-1)! possible permutations (we fix the starting city), this grows factorially. The algorithm: 1) Choose a starting city (typically city 0). 2) Generate all permutations of the remaining n-1 cities. 3) For each permutation, compute the total distance: distance from start to first city + distances between consecutive cities in permutation + distance from last city back to start. 4) Track the minimum distance found. Time complexity is O(n!) due to generating permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the current permutation and distances. This connects to the full TSP problem by providing an exact (though inefficient) solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to add the return trip to starting city, using 0-based vs 1-based indexing incorrectly, and not handling the case when n=1. The brute force approach is only practical for n ≤ 10-12 due to factorial growth.

examples.trace:
Starting city: 0. Permutations of [1,2]:
1) [1,2]: dist(0,1)=1 + dist(1,2)=3 + dist(2,0)=2 = 6
2) [2,1]: dist(0,2)=2 + dist(2,1)=3 + dist(1,0)=1 = 6
Minimum is 6

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings of n cities for path evaluation
ladders.explanation: The permutation generation is the core computational task in brute force TSP. We need to generate all possible orderings of cities to visit (excluding the starting city). The intuition is that each different ordering represents a unique path through all cities. We typically use backtracking/recursion or Heap's algorithm to generate permutations. The algorithm: 1) Start with a list of cities [1,2,...,n-1] (assuming 0 is fixed start). 2) Recursively generate permutations by swapping elements. 3) When we reach a complete permutation (all positions filled), process it (calculate distance). Time complexity is O(n!) since there are (n-1)! permutations. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and current permutation. This connects to L0 by providing the set of all possible paths to evaluate. Common mistakes include: generating permutations with duplicates (if cities aren't unique), not fixing the starting city, and inefficient generation algorithms. Heap's algorithm is efficient for permutation generation.

examples.trace:
Start: [1,2]
Swap position 0 with 0: [1,2] → process [1,2]
Swap position 0 with 1: [2,1] → process [2,1]
Total 2 permutations

Using Heap's algorithm:
Start: [1,2,3]
1) Process [1,2,3]
2) Swap 1↔2: [2,1,3], process [2,1,3]
3) Swap 1↔3: [3,1,2], process [3,1,2]
4) Swap 2↔3: [1,3,2], process [1,3,2]
5) Swap 1↔2: [2,3,1], process [2,3,1]
6) Swap 1↔3: [3,2,1], process [3,2,1]
Total 6 permutations

ladders.title: Core Logic — Path Distance Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate total distance for a given permutation of cities
ladders.explanation: Given a specific ordering of cities (a permutation) and a distance matrix, calculate the total travel distance for that path. The intuition is that we sum distances between consecutive cities in the path, plus the distances from start to first city and from last city back to start. This represents the complete cycle. The algorithm: 1) Start with total = 0. 2) Add distance from starting city (typically 0) to first city in permutation. 3) For each consecutive pair (i, i+1) in permutation, add distance between them. 4) Add distance from last city in permutation back to starting city. 5) Return total. Time complexity is O(n) for a path of length n. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the processing step for each generated permutation. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in indexing, forgetting the return trip, and not using the distance matrix correctly (ensuring symmetric distances if undirected).

examples.trace:
total = dist(0,1)=10
total += dist(1,2)=35 → total=45
total += dist(2,3)=30 → total=75
total += dist(3,0)=20 → total=95

total = dist(0,2)=2
total += dist(2,1)=3 → total=5
total += dist(1,0)=1 → total=6

ladders.title: Building Block — Working with Distance Matrices
ladders.desc: Understand and use 2D distance matrices to represent city connections
ladders.explanation: A distance matrix is a 2D array where dist[i][j] represents the distance from city i to city j. For TSP, this is typically symmetric (dist[i][j] = dist[j][i]) and has zeros on the diagonal (dist[i][i] = 0). The intuition is that this compact representation allows O(1) access to any distance between two cities. The matrix is usually n×n for n cities. Key operations: 1) Access distance between city i and j: dist[i][j]. 2) Verify symmetry if undirected graph. 3) Check for valid distances (non-negative). This connects to L2 by providing the data structure for distance lookup during path calculation. Common mistakes include: assuming 1-based indexing when matrix is 0-based, not handling asymmetric cases (if directed graph), and incorrect matrix dimensions.

examples.trace:
dist[0][2] = 15 (distance from city 0 to city 2)
dist[2][0] = 15 (symmetric)
Diagonal dist[0][0]=0, dist[1][1]=0, dist[2][2]=0

dist[1][0] = 5
dist[0][1] = 5 (symmetric)
Matrix is 2×2 for 2 cities

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Arrays/Lists
ladders.desc: Loop through collections to access and process elements
ladders.explanation: Iteration is fundamental to accessing each city in a path and each distance in calculations. The intuition is that we need to process sequences of cities one by one. Basic patterns: 1) For loop over indices: for i in range(len(path)): access path[i]. 2) For loop over elements: for city in path: use city directly. 3) Iterating consecutive pairs: for i in range(len(path)-1): use path[i] and path[i+1]. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by being how we access the distance matrix and path arrays. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., range(len(path)) vs range(len(path)-1)), modifying the list while iterating, and confusing indices with values.

examples.trace:
Using for city in path:
Iteration 1: city=1
Iteration 2: city=2
Iteration 3: city=3
Total 3 iterations

Using for i in range(len(path)-1):
i=0: path[0]=10, path[1]=20 → pair (10,20)
i=1: path[1]=20, path[2]=30 → pair (20,30)
i=2: path[2]=30, path[3]=40 → pair (30,40)
Total 3 pairs

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Permutations
ladders.desc: What is a permutation and how many exist for n items
ladders.explanation: A permutation is an arrangement of items in a specific order. For n distinct items, there are n! (n factorial) possible permutations. The intuition is: for the first position, you have n choices; for the second, n-1 choices (since one item is already used); continuing until last position has 1 choice. So total = n × (n-1) × ... × 1 = n!. Example: For 3 items A,B,C: permutations are ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA (6 total). This is foundational because TSP brute force needs to consider all possible orders of visiting cities. The number grows factorially: 1!=1, 2!=2, 3!=6, 4!=24, 5!=120, etc. This explains why brute force TSP becomes impractical for large n. Connection to L4: permutations are what we iterate through. Common mistakes: confusing permutations with combinations (where order doesn't matter), forgetting that items must be distinct for n! formula, and miscalculating factorial values.

examples.output: 6 permutations: ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA
examples.trace:
First position: 3 choices (A,B,C)
If first=A: second has 2 choices (B,C)
- If second=B: third=C → ABC
- If second=C: third=B → ACB
If first=B: second has 2 choices (A,C)
- If second=A: third=C → BAC
- If second=C: third=A → BCA
If first=C: second has 2 choices (A,B)
- If second=A: third=B → CAB
- If second=B: third=A → CBA
Total: 3×2×1=6

First position: 2 choices (X,Y)
If first=X: second=Y → XY
If first=Y: second=X → YX
Total: 2×1=2

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Bitmask O(n^2 * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use DP where state dp[mask][i] represents the minimum cost to visit cities in mask ending at city i. Iterate over masks and cities to build solutions. This is the standard optimal approach for exact TSP up to n around 20.

ladders.title: Full Problem — TSP with DP Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the traveling salesman problem using dynamic programming with bitmask state representation.
ladders.explanation: The traveling salesman problem asks: given n cities with distances between each pair, find the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. The DP bitmask approach solves this optimally for n up to about 20. The intuition is to represent subsets of visited cities as bitmasks (integers where bit i = 1 means city i is visited). We define dp[mask][i] as the minimum cost to visit all cities in mask ending at city i. We build up from smaller masks to larger ones. For each mask, for each ending city i in mask, we try all possible previous cities j in mask (j ≠ i) and compute dp[mask][i] = min(dp[mask_without_i][j] + dist[j][i]). The final answer is min over all i of dp[full_mask][i] + dist[i][0] to return to start. Time: O(n² * 2ⁿ) from nested loops over masks (2ⁿ) and cities (n²). Space: O(n * 2ⁿ) for dp table. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle the return trip, off-by-one in bitmask operations, not initializing dp[1<<0][0] = 0. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with INF, dp[1][0]=0. For mask from 1 to (1<<n)-1: for i in 0..n-1: if mask & (1<<i): for j in 0..n-1: if mask & (1<<j) and j≠i: dp[mask][i] = min(dp[mask][i], dp[mask^(1<<i)][j] + dist[j][i]). Answer = min_i(dp[(1<<n)-1][i] + dist[i][0]).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Transition
ladders.desc: Implement the core transition that builds dp[mask][i] from smaller masks.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dp[mask][i] = minimum cost to visit all cities in mask ending at city i. The intuition: to end at i with mask visited, we must have come from some city j that was the last city before i in a path covering mask_without_i. So we try all possible j in mask (j ≠ i) and take the minimum of dp[mask_without_i][j] + dist[j][i]. This works because dp[mask_without_i][j] already represents the optimal path covering those cities ending at j, and we simply extend it to i. Step-by-step: For a given mask and i where i is in mask (mask & (1<<i) ≠ 0), let prev_mask = mask ^ (1<<i) (remove i from mask). Initialize best = INF. For each j from 0 to n-1: if j is in prev_mask (prev_mask & (1<<j) ≠ 0) and j ≠ i, then candidate = dp[prev_mask][j] + dist[j][i]; update best = min(best, candidate). Set dp[mask][i] = best. Time: O(n) per state since we loop over j. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This is the heart of the DP that builds solutions in L0. Common mistakes: not checking j is in prev_mask, forgetting j≠i, using wrong distance matrix indices. Real code pattern: for mask in range(1<<n): for i in range(n): if mask & (1<<i): prev_mask = mask ^ (1<<i); best = INF; for j in range(n): if prev_mask & (1<<j): best = min(best, dp[prev_mask][j] + dist[j][i]); dp[mask][i] = best.

examples.input: n=3, dist=[[0,1,2],[1,0,3],[2,3,0]], dp[3][1]=1 (mask=011, cities {0,1}, end at 1), compute dp[7][2] (mask=111, all cities, end at 2)
examples.trace: Step 1: Given dp[3][1]=1 (path 0→1 cost 1). Step 2: To compute dp[7][2]: mask=111 (binary 111), i=2. prev_mask = 111 ^ (1<<2) = 111 ^ 100 = 011 (binary 011, cities {0,1}). Step 3: Try j=0: prev_mask has city 0 (011 & 001 ≠ 0), candidate = dp[011][0] + dist[0][2]. But dp[011][0] is INF (can't end at 0 with both 0 and1 visited), so skip. Step 4: Try j=1: prev_mask has city 1 (011 & 010 ≠ 0), candidate = dp[011][1] + dist[1][2] = 1 + 3 = 4. Step 5: best = 4, so dp[111][2] = 4.

examples.input: n=4, dist as in L0 example, dp[5][2]=15 (mask=101, cities {0,2}, end at 2), compute dp[13][3] (mask=1101, cities {0,2,3}, end at 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Given dp[5][2]=15. Step 2: Compute dp[13][3]: mask=1101 (binary 1101), i=3. prev_mask = 1101 ^ (1<<3) = 1101 ^ 1000 = 0101 (binary 0101, cities {0,2}). Step 3: Try j=0: prev_mask has city 0 (0101 & 0001 ≠ 0), candidate = dp[0101][0] + dist[0][3]. dp[0101][0] is INF, skip. Step 4: Try j=2: prev_mask has city 2 (0101 & 0100 ≠ 0), candidate = dp[0101][2] + dist[2][3] = 15 + 30 = 45. Step 5: best = 45, so dp[1101][3] = 45.

testCases.input: n=4, same dist, dp[3][1]=10, compute dp[7][2] (mask=0111)
n=4, same dist, dp[5][2]=15, compute dp[13][2] (mask=1101)
n=3, same dist, dp[3][1]=5, compute dp[7][2] (mask=111)
n=4, same dist, dp[3][1]=1, compute dp[7][2] (mask=0111)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using bitmasks to represent and manipulate subsets of cities.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to represent a subset of n cities as an n-bit integer (bitmask), where bit i is 1 if city i is in the subset, 0 otherwise. This allows O(1) subset operations and compact DP state representation. Intuition: There are 2ⁿ possible subsets of n cities, and integers 0 to 2ⁿ-1 naturally enumerate them via binary representation. For example, with n=4: mask=5 (binary 0101) represents cities {0,2} (bits 0 and 2 are 1). Key operations: 1) Check if city i is in mask: (mask >> i) & 1 or mask & (1<<i). 2) Add city i to mask: mask | (1<<i). 3) Remove city i from mask: mask & ~(1<<i) or mask ^ (1<<i). 4) Iterate over all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. Step-by-step: To build DP, we iterate masks in increasing order of size (or simply 0 to 2ⁿ-1 works as smaller masks are processed first if we check bits). For each mask, we can iterate over cities i where mask has bit i set. Time: O(2ⁿ * n) to iterate all masks and check bits. Space: O(1) for operations. Connection: This enables the DP state in L1. Common mistakes: off-by-one in bit shifts (1<<i not 1<<(i-1)), confusing mask indices with city indices. Real code pattern: n_cities = 4; for mask in range(1<<n_cities): for i in range(n_cities): if mask & (1<<i): # city i is in mask; new_mask = mask | (1<<j) to add city j; remove_mask = mask ^ (1<<i) to remove city i.

examples.trace: Step 1: Cities are indexed 0,1,2. Step 2: For subset {0,2}: bit 0 = 1, bit 1 = 0, bit 2 = 1. Step 3: Binary: 101. Step 4: Convert to decimal: 1*2² + 0*2¹ + 1*2⁰ = 4 + 0 + 1 = 5. Step 5: Operations: Check city 1: mask & (1<<1) = 5 & 2 = 4 (binary 101 & 010 = 000) = 0, so not in subset. Add city 1: mask | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7 (binary 101 | 010 = 111). Remove city 0: mask ^ (1<<0) = 5 ^ 1 = 4 (binary 101 ^ 001 = 100).

examples.input: n=4, mask=13 (binary 1101), list cities and remove city 2
examples.trace: Step 1: n=4, mask=13 decimal. Step 2: Binary: 13 = 1101 (bits: 3=1, 2=1, 1=0, 0=1). Step 3: Cities: bit 0=1 → city 0, bit 1=0 → city 1 not, bit 2=1 → city 2, bit 3=1 → city 3. So subset {0,2,3}. Step 4: Remove city 2: mask ^ (1<<2) = 13 ^ (1<<2) = 13 ^ 4 = 9. Step 5: Binary: 9 = 1001 (bits: city 0 and 3 only).

testCases.input: n=4, mask=6, list cities and add city 3

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Subsets in Increasing Size
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of n elements in order of their size (number of elements).
ladders.explanation: For DP to work correctly, we need to process subsets (bitmasks) in increasing order of their size (number of 1-bits) so that when computing dp[mask], all smaller subsets (prev_mask) are already computed. Intuition: The DP recurrence dp[mask][i] depends on dp[prev_mask][j] where prev_mask has one less city than mask. By processing masks by size, we ensure prev_mask is processed before mask. Step-by-step: 1) Compute the size of a mask: popcount(mask) = number of 1 bits. 2) Group masks by size from 1 to n (size 0 is empty set, ignored). 3) For each size s, iterate all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1, and process only those with popcount(mask) == s. Alternatively, iterate masks from 1 to (1<<n)-1 in natural order; since removing a bit reduces size, prev_mask always has smaller value if we iterate ascending, but explicit size order is clearer. Algorithm: for s in range(1, n+1): for mask in range(1<<n): if popcount(mask) == s: process mask. Popcount can be computed via built-in functions or bit tricks. Time: O(2ⁿ * n) if checking popcount naively, but we can optimize. Space: O(1). Connection: This ordering ensures correct DP computation in L2. Common mistakes: processing mask 0 (empty set) which isn't valid for TSP start, not handling size 1 initialization. Real code pattern: from collections import defaultdict; masks_by_size = defaultdict(list); for mask in range(1<<n): s = bin(mask).count('1'); if s>=1: masks_by_size[s].append(mask). Then for s in sorted(masks_by_size): for mask in masks_by_size[s]: process.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3, total masks 0 to 7. Step 2: Compute popcount: mask 0: 0 bits (size 0, skip). mask 1 (001): popcount=1 → size 1. mask 2 (010): popcount=1 → size 1. mask 3 (011): popcount=2 → size 2. mask 4 (100): popcount=1 → size 1. mask 5 (101): popcount=2 → size 2. mask 6 (110): popcount=2 → size 2. mask 7 (111): popcount=3 → size 3. Step 3: Group: Size 1: masks 1,2,4. Size 2: 3,5,6. Size 3: 7.

examples.input: n=2, process masks in size order
examples.trace: Step 1: n=2, masks 0-3. Step 2: Size 1: mask 1 (01), mask 2 (10). Size 2: mask 3 (11). Step 3: Order: first mask 1 (city 0), then mask 2 (city 1), then mask 3 (cities 0 and 1). This ensures when processing mask 3, masks 1 and 2 (its subsets) are done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation: Set, Check, Toggle Bits
ladders.desc: Fundamental bit operations to manipulate individual bits in an integer.
ladders.explanation: Bit manipulation uses bitwise operators to work with individual bits of integers, which is essential for bitmask DP. Intuition: An integer is stored in binary; each bit can represent a boolean state (e.g., city visited). Operators: 1) Left shift (<<): 1 << i creates a number with only bit i set to 1 (e.g., 1<<2 = 4). 2) Bitwise OR (|): sets bits to 1 if either operand has 1. Used to add a bit: mask | (1<<i). 3) Bitwise AND (&): checks if a bit is set: (mask & (1<<i)) != 0. 4) Bitwise XOR (^): toggles a bit: mask ^ (1<<i) flips bit i. 5) Bitwise NOT (~): inverts all bits, but careful with Python's infinite bits. Step-by-step: To work with n bits, we use integers up to 2ⁿ-1. Example: mask = 5 (binary 101). Check bit 1: mask & (1<<1) = 5 & 2 = 0 → false. Set bit 1: mask | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7 (111). Toggle bit 0: mask ^ (1<<0) = 5 ^ 1 = 4 (100). Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection: These operations build the bitmask handling in L3. Common mistakes: using ^ to add a bit (it toggles, so if bit already 1, it removes it), misunderstanding operator precedence (use parentheses). Real code pattern: mask = 0; mask |= (1<<i) # set bit i; if mask & (1<<j): # check bit j; mask ^= (1<<k) # toggle bit k.

examples.input: mask=6 (binary 110), check bit 0, set bit 0, toggle bit 1
examples.trace: Step 1: mask=6, binary 110. Step 2: Check bit 0: mask & (1<<0) = 6 & 1 = 0 (110 & 001 = 000) → false. Step 3: Set bit 0: mask | (1<<0) = 6 | 1 = 7 (110 | 001 = 111). Step 4: Now mask=7, binary 111. Toggle bit 1: mask ^ (1<<1) = 7 ^ 2 = 5 (111 ^ 010 = 101).

examples.input: mask=9 (binary 1001), check bit 2, set bit 2, toggle bit 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bits
ladders.desc: Understanding binary representation of integers and what bits are.
ladders.explanation: Binary is base-2 numbering system where each digit (bit) is 0 or 1. Intuition: Computers store integers in binary; a bit is the smallest unit of data. An n-bit number can represent 2ⁿ values from 0 to 2ⁿ-1. For example, 3-bit numbers: 000=0, 001=1, 010=2, 011=3, 100=4, 101=5, 110=6, 111=7. Each bit position has a place value: from right (least significant bit, position 0) to left, values are 2⁰=1, 2¹=2, 2²=4, etc. To convert binary to decimal: sum (bit * 2ⁱ) for i from 0. To convert decimal to binary: repeatedly divide by 2, collect remainders. Step-by-step: Example: binary 1011: bits from right: 1 (2⁰), 1 (2¹), 0 (2²), 1 (2³) = 1+2+0+8=11 decimal. Example: decimal 6 to binary: 6/2=3 rem 0, 3/2=1 rem 1, 1/2=0 rem 1 → read backwards: 110. Time: O(log n) for conversions. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the prerequisite for bit operations in L4. Common mistakes: confusing bit positions (0-indexed from right), misplacing place values. Real code pattern: In Python, bin(5) gives '0b101', int('101',2) gives 5.

examples.trace: Step 1: Binary 1101 has 4 bits. Step 2: From right (position 0): bit 0 = 1, value = 1 * 2⁰ = 1. Step 3: Bit 1 = 0, value = 0 * 2¹ = 0. Step 4: Bit 2 = 1, value = 1 * 2² = 4. Step 5: Bit 3 = 1, value = 1 * 2³ = 8. Step 6: Sum: 1 + 0 + 4 + 8 = 13.

Step 1: Start with 9. Step 2: 9 divided by 2: quotient 4, remainder 1. Step 3: 4 divided by 2: quotient 2, remainder 0. Step 4: 2 divided by 2: quotient 1, remainder 0. Step 5: 1 divided by 2: quotient 0, remainder 1. Step 6: Read remainders backwards: 1,0,0,1 → binary 1001.
